message.content:
Adversarial verifier, HC-GRC agent/pipeline deep audit. Repo /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-grc. REFUTE this candidate by reading the real code AND the cited spec. Remember: an intentional Phase-0/1 stub (NotImplementedError + IMPLEMENTED=False) is NOT a defect — set is_stub_by_design=true and confirmed=false for those. Default confirmed=false unless it is a genuine defect in implemented logic, a real research-integrity hole, or a real code-vs-spec contradiction.

CANDIDATE (unit xc-protected-flags):
title: GATES.md protected agent set has stale agent names
severity: medium
location: docs/architecture/GATES.md, lines 28-34
problem: The 'Protected Agent Set' table lists the same four agents by obsolete names: p2-domain-clustering, p3-coverage-diffusion, p4-gap-analysis, p5-risk-quantification. This documentation is a barrier to Agent Evolution correctly binding the protection guard to the real agents.
spec_or_invariant: GATES.md §77 mirrors ADR-0015's protected agent set. Both must be synchronized and must list the canonical AGENT_ID values from the agent implementations. ADR-0015 Consequences (line 171) explicitly requires GATES.md to be updated with corrected names.
proposed_fix: Update GATES.md protected agent table (lines 28-34) to use the canonical names: p2-control-topology, p3-regulatory-convergence, p4-risk-blindspot, p5-ai-governance.

Verify against actual code + spec (read-only). Cite file:line and the exact spec clause. Be honest about severity.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll conduct a thorough adversarial verification of this candidate by examining the actual code, documentation, and specifications.
Now let me read the GATES.md file and the referenced spec (ADR-0015):

content.content:
1	# Human Approval Gates
2	
3	**Status:** Locked — gate topology is fixed at pre-registration.
4	
5	---
6	
7	## Phase 0 Prerequisites — Structural Firewall Preconditions
8	
9	All structural integrity claims in the project documentation are conditional on Phase 0 implementation completing before any data acquisition. The following must be verified before SCF data enters the system (per ADR-0015, #71):
10	
11	- [ ] LangGraph skeleton running with all five gate nodes present
12	- [ ] Gate 1 fires with synthetic payload and produces a valid gate record
13	- [ ] PostgresSaver checkpointing confirmed on Mac Mini compute node
14	- [ ] `compute_data_split()` idempotency test passes: two runs with identical state produce bit-identical output (seed derived from SHA-256 of data manifest hash — see #75)
15	- [ ] Hardware benchmark complete: PostgresSaver write throughput characterized, `batch_size` locked (see #76)
16	- [ ] Governance dry-run complete: all five components passing (see #93)
17	
18	**No SCF data may be acquired until all items above are checked.**
19	
20	---
21	
22	## Protected Agent Set — Agent Evolution Constraint
23	
24	The following agents' prompts may not be modified by Agent Evolution autonomously. Any modification requires Escalation approval before taking effect (per ADR-0015, #77):
25	
26	| Agent | Role |
27	|-------|------|
28	| p1-strm-nlp | NLP similarity measurement |
29	| p2-domain-clustering | Clustering methodology |
30	| p3-coverage-diffusion | Semantic coverage measurement |
31	| p4-gap-analysis | Gap analysis methodology |
32	| p5-risk-quantification | Risk quantification approach |
33	| statistical-analyst | Evidence weighting and testing |
34	| hypothesis-formalizer | Hypothesis structure for testing |
35	
36	Agent Evolution retains full autonomous scope over all non-protected agents.
37	
38	---
39	
40	## Gate Coordinator Node
41	
42	All `gate_status` and `gate_decisions` writes are routed exclusively through a single serialized gate coordinator node. No agent writes directly to these keys. Enforced by LangGraph edge definitions (per ADR-0015, #74).
43	
44	HC-GRC enforces five human approval gates implemented as LangGraph `interrupt()` calls in the Orchestrator node. Gates are not advisory — they are structural. The pipeline cannot advance past a gate without an explicit human decision recorded in the run manifest and the pre-registration ledger.
45	
46	---
47	
48	## Engineering Requirements (LangGraph interrupt() Rules)
49	
50	These are correctness requirements, not guidelines. Violating them silently breaks checkpoint replay. Every engineer and agent working on gate implementation must read this section.
51	
52	**Rule 1: Do not wrap `interrupt()` calls in try/except.**
53	Swallowed exceptions during interrupt prevent LangGraph from recording the interrupt in the checkpoint. On replay, the graph skips the gate entirely. This failure is silent on the first run and observable only on re-run.
54	
55	**Rule 2: Do not reorder `interrupt()` calls within a node.**
56	Resumption from a checkpoint is index-based. If a node contains two interrupt calls and their order is swapped between the original run and a replay, the second human response is delivered to the first gate. The failure is logically silent — the graph resumes, but with the wrong gate decision applied.
57	
58	**Rule 3: Do not return complex objects from `interrupt()`.**
59	Return only serializable primitives. Complex objects may fail to checkpoint correctly, producing a gate that appears to have been approved but whose decision cannot be verified on replay.
60	
61	**Rule 4: Side effects before `interrupt()` must be idempotent.**
62	LangGraph may re-execute a node on replay. Any side effect that ran before the interrupt (writing to the lab notebook, emitting a provenance record) runs again. If those operations are not idempotent, replays produce duplicate records or inconsistent state.
63	
64	**Implementation note:** LangGraph documentation recommends `durability: "sync"` for high-durability cases (gate-critical pipeline). This ensures checkpoints are written before execution continues. Set this in the PostgresSaver configuration for all gate nodes.
65	
66	**Human response format:** Gate decisions must be provided as `approve | edit | reject | respond`. Decisions are delivered via `Command(resume=...)` and must be in the same order as the `interrupt()` calls appear in the gate node.
67	
68	---
69	
70	## Gate Inventory
71	
72	| Gate | ID | Phase Transition | Trigger Condition | Decision Types |
73	|------|-----|-----------------|-------------------|----------------|
74	| Gate 1 | `gate-1` | Pre-analysis → Exploratory | Protocol documents reviewed and locked | approve / reject |
75	| Gate 2 | `gate-2` | Exploratory → Confirmatory | SAP finalized, hypotheses registered, test split released | approve / reject |
76	| Gate 3 | `gate-3` | Confirmatory → Review | All pre-specified confirmatory tests complete | approve / reject / edit |
77	| Gate 4 | `gate-4` | Review → Dissemination | QA Agent scores all findings ≥ 3.5/5 | approve / reject |
78	| Gate 5 | `gate-5` | Dissemination → Archive | External publication or release | approve / reject |
79	
80	---
81	
82	## Gate Definitions
83	
84	### Gate 1 — Protocol Lock
85	**Unlocks:** Exploratory analysis on train + validation splits.
86	**Requires before approval:**
87	- `00_research_questions.md` — platform-level ERQs complete
88	- `01_theoretical_framework.md` — theoretical claims and construct definitions locked
89	- `04_methods_scaffolding.md` — test families, split strategy, firewall rules locked
90	- Pre-registration commit on protected branch with RFC 3161 timestamp
91	- Data acquisition complete — SCF downloaded, SHA-256 verified, DVC versioned
92	
93	**Data access change:** None — test split remains locked.
94	**Cannot approve if:** Any protocol document is in draft state. Pre-registration commit missing.
95	
96	### Gate 2 — Confirmatory Lock
97	**Unlocks:** Confirmatory analysis on the held-out test split. Releases test split decryption key.
98	**Requires before approval:**
99	- Exploratory analysis complete — all five ERQs characterized
100	- `03_statistical_analysis_plan.md` — specific hypotheses (H[module].[n]), named tests, effect size thresholds, correction methods all locked
101	- SAP pre-registration commit on protected branch with RFC 3161 timestamp
102	- Theoretical framework amendment committed (TC1–TC4 → specific hypotheses)
103	- Human reviewer has read and approved the SAP
104	
105	**Data access change:** Test split becomes accessible. This transition is irreversible.
106	**Cannot approve if:** SAP is incomplete. Any hypothesis lacks a named test or effect size. Exploratory analysis ran on the test split (manifest `test_split_accessed == true` for any prior exploratory run).
107	
108	### Gate 3 — Analysis Review
109	**Unlocks:** Dissemination preparation (writing agents).
110	**Requires before approval:**
111	- All pre-specified confirmatory tests complete on test split
112	- Statistical Analyst outputs reviewed
113	- QA Agent has scored all findings
114	- Null results documented with equal rigor as positive results
115	- Run manifest complete for all confirmatory runs
116	
117	**Cannot approve if:** Any pre-specified hypothesis test is missing. QA Agent has not scored all findings.
118	
119	### Gate 4 — Dissemination Authorization
120	**Unlocks:** ML Paper Agent, Whitepaper Agent, Business Presentation Agent, and all dissemination agents.
121	**Requires before approval:**
122	- All findings have QA score ≥ 3.5/5
123	- All findings trace to pre-registered hypotheses
124	- Limitations section covers all QA-flagged concerns
125	- Branding Compliance Agent pre-review for any practitioner-facing outputs
126	
127	**Cannot approve if:** Any finding with QA score < 3.5/5 without documented escalation reason. Any finding without SAP trace.
128	
129	### Gate 5 — Archive and Release
130	**Unlocks:** External publication or public release of findings.
131	**Requires before approval:**
132	- License Compliance Agent review complete
133	- IP Attribution Agent sign-off — all attributions present including SCF CC BY-ND
134	- Branding Compliance Agent final review
135	- Human reviewer has read final outputs
136	
137	---
138	
139	## Dissent Logging Requirement
140	
141	Gate decisions are recorded in two places: the run manifest `gate_decisions[]` array and the pre-registration ledger. Both record `outcome`, `reviewer`, `timestamp`, `rationale`, and `dissent`.
142	
143	`dissent` is a required field in both records. `null` means no dissent — it is not the same as an absent field. A gate record with no `dissent` field fails manifest validation.
144	
145	**Rationale for this rule:** Adversarial collaboration research (Isch et al. 2025) documents that approval gates converge on consensus when the same reviewer never records dissent. A dissent field that is always `null` is a signal, not a validation failure — but it is a signal that should trigger review of whether the gate is functioning as a real check.
146	
147	Gate rejections must include a rationale of at least 20 words. A rejection with a short rationale ("insufficient quality") fails manifest validation. The rationale must name the specific deficiency.
148	
149	---
150	
151	## Agents Passing Through Each Gate
152	
153	*Auto-generated from AGENT.md Handoffs sections. Regenerate with `make generate-docs`.*
154	
155	| Gate | Agents That Trigger It |
156	|------|----------------------|
157	| Gate 1 | Orchestrator (protocol lock check) |
158	| Gate 2 | Orchestrator (SAP lock + exploratory complete check) |
159	| Gate 3 | Orchestrator (confirmatory analysis complete check) |
160	| Gate 4 | QA Agent → Orchestrator |
161	| Gate 5 | Branding Compliance Agent → Orchestrator |
162	
163	*Note: This table is currently hand-authored. Once `tools/generate_docs.py` is implemented per CARDS_SPEC.md, this section is auto-generated.*
164

content.text: Now let me read ADR-0015 to check the spec and consequences:

content.content:
1	# ADR-0015: Architecture Integrity Decisions
2	
3	**Status:** Accepted  
4	**Date:** 2026-06-10  
5	**Author:** Thomas Jones  
6	**Supersedes:** N/A  
7	**Relates to:** ADR-0011 (resilience), ADR-0012 (autonomous architecture), ADR-0014 (infrastructure)
8	
9	---
10	
11	## Context
12	
13	Adversarial review surfaced ten architecture integrity issues (#71–#80) spanning: structural firewall implementation, orchestrator failure model, state schema safety, data split integrity, checkpoint scale, agent evolution scope, inter-agent trust, observability correlation, and gate delivery mechanism. This ADR captures the resolution of all ten.
14	
15	---
16	
17	## Decisions
18	
19	### 1. Structural Firewall Claims Are Conditional on Phase 0 (#71)
20	
21	All claims that "the firewall between phases is structural, not procedural" — in ADR-0004, ADR-0007, README, and the Charter — are conditional on Phase 0 implementation completing before any data acquisition.
22	
23	**Phase 0 success criteria (hard prerequisites, added to GATES.md):**
24	- LangGraph skeleton running with all gate nodes present
25	- Gate 1 firing with synthetic payload confirmed
26	- PostgresSaver checkpointing confirmed on target hardware
27	- Gate 2 data split logic implemented and idempotency test passing
28	- No SCF data acquired until all criteria are met
29	
30	The structural firewall claim becomes true when Phase 0 completes. Not before.
31	
32	---
33	
34	### 2. Orchestrator Failure Model — Option B + Evolve Layer (#72)
35	
36	**Option B — Defensive gate nodes:** Each gate validates its own preconditions and rejects invalid state regardless of how T-00 routed. Gate nodes are the enforcement surface.
37	
38	**Evolve layer:** Gate rejection events are logged with full context (proposed action, rejection reason, state snapshot, run_id, timestamp) to a `failure_events` store. Agent Evolution monitors `failure_events` for patterns:
39	
40	- **Routing errors** (T-00 operational): Agent Evolution applies DSPy re-optimization to T-00's routing prompt autonomously. Logged to PROV-DM with before/after versions.
41	- **Architectural failures** (gate topology wrong, systematic design flaw): Escalation filed to operator.
42	
43	This makes the platform self-correcting for operational routing failures without requiring a permanent watchdog node. Option A (real-time watchdog) becomes redundant once T-00's routing stabilizes through the evolve loop.
44	
45	**Protected agent set:** Agent Evolution may not autonomously modify P1–P5, statistical-analyst, or hypothesis-formalizer prompts. See Decision 6.
46	
47	---
48	
49	### 3. Provenance Entry Ordering (#73)
50	
51	Every entry appended to `prov_trace`, `errors`, and `null_results` carries:
52	- `timestamp`: ISO 8601 UTC, generated at write time by the writing agent
53	- `source_id`: agent name + run_id + superstep number
54	
55	Downstream consumers sort by `timestamp` before processing. List position is never used as a proxy for execution order. Enforced by ARA Rigor Reviewer at validation time.
56	
57	---
58	
59	### 4. Gate Status Write Protection (#74)
60	
61	All `gate_status` writes go through `gate_coordinator_node`. No other node writes this key directly.
62	
63	**Phase 0 (linear topology) — implemented via merge reducer:**
64	`gate_status` is typed `Annotated[dict[GateID, GateStatus], lambda a, b: {**a, **b}]`. Each `gate_coordinator_node` call returns only the new `{gate_id: record}` entry; LangGraph merges it into the existing dict. This is safe under concurrent writes because dict-merge is commutative for non-overlapping keys (each gate writes a unique `gate_id`).
65	
66	`gate_coordinator_node` is a helper function called from within gate nodes — it is **not** a standalone LangGraph node in Phase 0. The ADR was written before implementation; the merge reducer approach was selected during adversarial review (F1/F4, 2026-06-11) as the correct Phase 0 mechanism.
67	
68	**Phase 1 (parallel topology) — to be implemented:**
69	When Phase 1 introduces parallel agent execution, the merge reducer alone cannot prevent two agents writing the same `gate_id` key simultaneously. Phase 1 will promote `gate_coordinator_node` to a true serialized LangGraph node with explicit edges from all gate-writing agents. Tracked in GitHub issue #103.
70	
71	**Topology enforcement (Decision 7 / #78) is a Phase 1 goal, not a Phase 0 guarantee.**
72	
73	---
74	
75	### 5. Data Split Idempotency — Deterministic Seed (#75)
76	
77	`compute_data_split()` derives its random seed exclusively from SHA-256 of the data manifest hash. No system entropy is used (no time-based seed, `os.urandom()`, or unseeded numpy random).
78	
79	**Required test (Phase 0 success criterion):** Run `compute_data_split()` twice with identical state. Assert bit-identical output. This test must pass before Gate 2 is considered operational.
80	
81	---
82	
83	### 6. Checkpoint Scale — Phase 0 Benchmark (#76)
84	
85	Hardware benchmarking is a Phase 0 deliverable, not a Phase 3 decision. Before Phase 3 implementation begins:
86	
87	1. Estimate checkpoint size per `HCGRCState` at analysis time
88	2. Benchmark PostgresSaver write throughput on Mac Mini compute node
89	3. Derive maximum feasible parallel branch count
90	4. Select `batch_size` accordingly — lock to SWARM_IMPLEMENTATION_ROADMAP.md
91	
92	`batch_size=50` is a placeholder until the benchmark runs. The result may require architectural adjustment.
93	
94	---
95	
96	### 7. Agent Evolution Scope — Protected Agent Set (#77)
97	
98	**Protected agents** (prompt modifications require Escalation approval before taking effect):
99	
100	| Agent | Reason |
101	|-------|--------|
102	| p1-strm-nlp | Defines NLP similarity measurement approach |
103	| p2-domain-clustering | Defines clustering methodology |
104	| p3-coverage-diffusion | Defines semantic coverage measurement |
105	| p4-gap-analysis | Defines gap analysis methodology |
106	| p5-risk-quantification | Defines risk quantification approach |
107	| statistical-analyst | Defines how evidence is weighted and tested |
108	| hypothesis-formalizer | Defines how hypotheses are structured for testing |
109	
110	Modifying these agents' prompts mid-tier against SCF corpus data is analytically equivalent to changing the research design post-hoc — a form of data snooping that bypasses Gate 2.
111	
112	**Legitimate evolution paths for protected agents:**
113	
114	1. **Before Gate 1:** DSPy optimization against synthetic data or public benchmarks only. No SCF data in system yet.
115	2. **Mid-tier via Escalation:** Platform files structured proposal with specific change and rationale. Operator reviews via Claude. Decision logged before change takes effect.
116	3. **Between tiers:** Post-tier retrospective informs deliberate redesign. Changes committed and version-bumped before next tier starts. This is science working correctly — Tier 1 findings inform Tier 2 instrument design.
117	
118	**Agent Evolution scope:** Fully autonomous for all non-protected agents (T-00 routing, ingestion, reporting, QA, infrastructure).
119	
120	---
121	
122	### 8. Inter-Agent Trust — Topology Enforcement (#78)
123	
124	**Phase 1 — Option A (structural):** LangGraph edge definitions control which agents connect to which state keys. An agent not wired to write a particular key cannot reach it — enforced at graph compile time, zero runtime overhead.
125	
126	**Phase 2 — Option B (runtime assertions):** Deferred. Tracked in sub-issue #96. Activate when: a state corruption event occurs that topology enforcement did not catch, or Tier 2 scale requires finer-grained write control.
127	
128	---
129	
130	### 9. Observability Correlation — Common run_id (#79)
131	
132	A single `run_id` is generated at the start of each research run and propagated to all four observability stores:
133	
134	| Store | Integration |
135	|-------|------------|
136	| MLflow | `run_id` as experiment tag |
137	| Phoenix | `run_id` as trace attribute |
138	| W3C PROV-DM | `run_id` as activity identifier |
139	| PostgresSaver | `run_id` as thread_id / checkpoint key |
140	
141	Cross-store correlation is a single-key lookup. A `RUNBOOK.md` documents the explicit cross-store query sequence for common diagnostic scenarios. No custom query infrastructure built.
142	
143	---
144	
145	### 10. Gate 2 Delivery — Escalation Loop (#80)
146	
147	Gate 2 interrupt uses the Escalation loop established in ADR-0014. No separate UI required.
148	
149	The Gate 2 payload (split parameters, summary statistics) travels via GitHub issue — not raw SCF data. Data stays on the compute node. The operator reviews summary artifacts, not the dataset. Local-first constraint (ADR-0002) maintained.
150	
151	---
152	
153	## Consequences
154	
155	**What this enables:**
156	- Phase 0 can proceed with a complete, unambiguous success criteria checklist
157	- Orchestrator failures are self-correcting without permanent watchdog overhead
158	- Gate integrity is structurally enforced, not convention-dependent
159	- Research instrument (P1–P5) integrity is protected against silent prompt drift
160	- Cross-store diagnostics are operationally feasible for a single-operator program
161	- Gate 2 approval requires no new infrastructure
162	
163	**What this closes:**
164	- #71 through #80: all ten adversarial architecture findings resolved
165	
166	**What this creates:**
167	- #96: runtime assertion guards (Phase 2, sub-issue of #78)
168	- Phase 0 success criteria expanded with: idempotency test, hardware benchmark, Gate 1 structural test
169	
170	**Documents requiring update:**
171	- GATES.md: Phase 0 prerequisites, protected agent set, gate coordinator node
172	- SWARM_IMPLEMENTATION_ROADMAP.md: batch_size benchmark as Phase 0 deliverable
173	- Agent Evolution AGENT.md: protected agent set constraint
174	- P1–P5 and statistical-analyst, hypothesis-formalizer AGENT.md cards: behavioral constraint on prompt modification
175	- RUNBOOK.md: create with common run_id cross-store query procedures
176

content.text: Good. Now I need to find the actual agent implementations to determine their canonical AGENT_ID values. Let me search for agent definitions:
Let me look for the specific P-tier agents mentioned in the candidate. I need to find p2, p3, p4, and p5 agents:

input.command: find /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-grc -type d -name "p2-*" -o -type d -name "p3-*" -o -type d -name "p4-*" -o -type d -name "p5-*" 2>/dev/null | head -20

content.text: Excellent! The actual agent directories exist with the NEW names. Let me verify the AGENT.md files to confirm the canonical AGENT_ID values:

content.content:
1	---
2	name: p2-control-topology
3	description: Analyzes the structural topology of the SCF control space as a graph — mapping control relationships as edges, detecting clusters, identifying hub controls, measuring domain connectivity, and characterizing the mathematical properties of the control network. Produces the first formal graph-theoretic characterization of a security metaframework at this scale.
4	version: 1.0.0
5	team: 03-analysis
6	status: primary
7	trigger: always
8	author: HC-GRC
9	tags: [P2, Graph Analysis, Network Topology, Control Space, Clustering, Community Detection]
10	skills: [ray-data]
11	tools: [mcp-qdrant, mcp-mlflow, mcp-lab-notebook]
12	---
13	
14	# P2 Agent — Control Space Topology
15	
16	## Purpose
17	
18	1,400 controls connected by 280,000 mappings form a graph. That graph has properties — hubs, bridges, isolated clusters, densely connected communities — that are invisible when you read controls one at a time. P2 makes those properties visible and measurable. Which controls are most central to the entire framework? Which domains are structurally isolated from the rest? Where are the bridges between apparently unrelated compliance regimes? The topology findings directly inform P3 (convergence), P4 (risk blindspots), and P5 (governance clustering).
19	
20	## Position in Workflow
21	
22	Runs in parallel with P1 after Embedding Agent and Gate 2. Topology analysis is largely exploratory; confirmatory hypotheses focus on specific structural predictions.
23	
24	```
25	[Embedding Agent: Qdrant collections]
26	[Data Steward: splits]
27	        ↓
28	[P2 Agent]
29	  ├── Graph construction from STRM mappings
30	  ├── EXP_ topology metrics (degree, centrality, clustering coefficient)
31	  ├── Community detection (Leiden, Louvain)
32	  └── H2.x confirmatory tests
33	        ↓
34	[P3 Agent] and [P4 Agent] and [Statistical Analyst]
35	```
36	
37	## Inputs
38	
39	| Input | Source | Format | Schema / Notes |
40	|-------|--------|--------|----------------|
41	| STRM mappings | data/splits/ | Parquet | Edges: source control, target control, relationship type, strength |
42	| Control metadata | ara/artifacts/ | JSON | Node attributes: domain, framework, maturity level, MCR/DSR classification |
43	| Registered hypotheses | docs/protocol/03_statistical_analysis_plan.md | JSON | H2.x hypotheses with test specifications |
44	
45	## Outputs / Artifacts
46	
47	| Artifact | Destination | Format | Schema / Notes |
48	|----------|-------------|--------|----------------|
49	| Control graph | analysis/01-exploratory/EXP_P2_graph.graphml | GraphML | Nodes: controls; edges: STRM mappings with type + strength attributes |
50	| Topology metrics | analysis/01-exploratory/EXP_P2_topology.parquet | Parquet | Degree, betweenness centrality, clustering coefficient, PageRank per control |
51	| Community assignments | analysis/01-exploratory/EXP_P2_communities.parquet | Parquet | Community ID, size, intra/inter-community edge density |
52	| Hub control register | analysis/01-exploratory/EXP_P2_hubs.md | Markdown | Top 50 most central controls with domain and framework annotations |
53	| Confirmatory results | analysis/02-confirmatory/H2.x_results.parquet | Parquet | Per-hypothesis test results |
54	
55	## Tools & MCP Servers
56	
57	| Tool | Purpose | MCP Server | Notes |
58	|------|---------|-----------|-------|
59	| Qdrant | Query control metadata for node attributes | mcp-qdrant | Metadata filtering by domain, framework, maturity |
60	| MLflow | Track graph construction parameters and metrics | mcp-mlflow | Graph density, N nodes, N edges per run |
61	| Lab notebook | Record topology findings and anomalies | mcp-lab-notebook | Append-only |
62	
63	## Skills Used
64	
65	- **Ray Data** — Parallel graph construction and metric computation across 280,000+ edges. Centrality computations parallelized via Ray for performance on dense graphs.
66	
67	## Handoffs
68	
69	**Receives from**: Data Steward (splits), Embedding Agent (Qdrant metadata), ARA artifacts (node attributes)
70	**Passes to**: P3 Agent (community assignments for cross-framework analysis), P4 Agent (hub map for blindspot detection), Statistical Analyst (H2.x test inputs), Report Agent (topology visualizations)
71	**Human gate**: Gate 3 — human reviews community structure and hub control register before confirmatory analysis
72	
73	## Behavioral Constraints
74	- **Protected research agent:** This agent's prompts may not be modified autonomously by Agent Evolution. Any prompt modification requires Escalation approval before taking effect. Modifying this agent mid-tier against SCF corpus data constitutes a research design change that bypasses Gate 2 (per ADR-0015, #77).
75	
76	- Graph construction uses STRM relationship types as directed edge types — subset (⊂) and superset (⊃) edges are directed; equivalence (=) and intersection (∩) are undirected.
77	- NIST Cluster Independence Constraint applies — NIST 800-53, CSF, 800-171 node attributes are tagged as one authorship cluster in community analysis.
78	- Strength scores used as edge weights; missing strength scores imputed at median (documented in codebook).
79	- All exploratory graph metrics labeled EXP_ — no confirmatory claims from topology exploration.
80	
81	## Failure Modes & Recovery
82	
83	| Failure | Detection | Recovery |
84	|---------|-----------|----------|
85	| Graph too sparse for community detection | Edge density < 0.001 | Log finding; report as structural characteristic; do not force community structure |
86	| Centrality computation timeout on full graph | > 30min runtime | Use approximate centrality (sampling); log approximation parameters |
87	| Disconnected graph components | N components > 1 | Analyze largest component; document isolated controls separately |
88	
89	## Evaluation Criteria
90	
91	- [ ] Graph constructed from 100% of STRM mappings in analysis split
92	- [ ] Community detection run with at least 2 algorithms (Leiden + Louvain) for robustness
93	- [ ] Hub controls validated against domain expert expectations (QA Agent spot check)
94	- [ ] NIST cluster constraint documented in graph metadata
95	- [ ] All confirmatory scripts have SAP headers validated by Code Review Agent
96	
97	## Notes
98	
99	The graph is both a research artifact and an analytical substrate. P3, P4, and P5 all query the P2 graph structure. The GraphML export is versioned in DVC and referenced by downstream agents — any re-run of P2 that changes graph structure requires all downstream modules to re-run as well.
100

1	---
2	name: p3-regulatory-convergence
3	description: Quantifies cross-framework regulatory convergence across the 33 SCF domains — measuring the degree to which different compliance frameworks (NIST, ISO, SOC2, PCI-DSS, HIPAA, etc.) agree, overlap, or diverge in their control requirements. Produces the Regulatory Convergence Atlas: a quantified map of where frameworks are genuinely aligned vs. superficially similar.
4	version: 1.0.0
5	team: 03-analysis
6	status: primary
7	trigger: always
8	author: HC-GRC
9	tags: [P3, Regulatory Convergence, Cross-Framework Analysis, NIST, ISO, Compliance Mapping]
10	skills: [sentence-transformers, instructor]
11	tools: [mcp-qdrant, mcp-mlflow, mcp-lab-notebook]
12	---
13	
14	# P3 Agent — Regulatory Convergence Atlas
15	
16	## Purpose
17	
18	GRC practitioners spend enormous effort manually mapping requirements across frameworks — assuming that similar-sounding requirements from different frameworks are genuinely equivalent. P3 tests that assumption at scale. It measures actual semantic and structural convergence between framework pairs across all 33 domains, identifies where convergence is real vs. terminological coincidence, and maps the genuine divergences that practitioners currently miss. The output is actionable: organizations can use the convergence atlas to identify which frameworks genuinely satisfy others and which require independent controls despite apparent overlap.
19	
20	## Position in Workflow
21	
22	Runs after P2 community structure is available. Depends on P2's cross-framework community assignments to identify candidate convergence clusters.
23	
24	```
25	[P2 Agent: community assignments, hub controls]
26	[Embedding Agent: Qdrant cross-framework queries]
27	        ↓
28	[P3 Agent]
29	  ├── Pairwise framework convergence metrics
30	  ├── Domain-level convergence heatmap
31	  ├── Genuine vs. terminological convergence classification
32	  └── H3.x confirmatory tests
33	        ↓
34	[P4 Agent] and [Statistical Analyst] and [Report Agent]
35	```
36	
37	## Inputs
38	
39	| Input | Source | Format | Schema / Notes |
40	|-------|--------|--------|----------------|
41	| Community structure | P2 Agent output | Parquet | Cross-framework community assignments |
42	| Framework-tagged controls | ara/artifacts/ + Qdrant | JSON + Vector | Controls with framework, domain, maturity metadata |
43	| STRM equivalence mappings | data/splits/ | Parquet | Relationship type = (equivalence) mappings as convergence candidates |
44	| Framework relationships | docs/charter/FRAMEWORK_RELATIONSHIPS.md | Markdown | Known NIST cluster constraint and documented framework genealogies |
45	
46	## Outputs / Artifacts
47	
48	| Artifact | Destination | Format | Schema / Notes |
49	|----------|-------------|--------|----------------|
50	| Convergence matrix | analysis/01-exploratory/EXP_P3_convergence.parquet | Parquet | Framework × framework × domain: convergence score 0–1 |
51	| Convergence atlas | analysis/01-exploratory/EXP_P3_atlas.json | JSON | Structured: genuine convergence clusters, false convergence flags, divergence regions |
52	| Domain-level heatmap data | analysis/01-exploratory/EXP_P3_heatmap.parquet | Parquet | Input to Visualization Agent |
53	| Confirmatory results | analysis/02-confirmatory/H3.x_results.parquet | Parquet | Per-hypothesis test results |
54	
55	## Tools & MCP Servers
56	
57	| Tool | Purpose | MCP Server | Notes |
58	|------|---------|-----------|-------|
59	| Qdrant | Cross-framework semantic similarity queries | mcp-qdrant | Metadata filter: framework pairs, domain |
60	| MLflow | Track convergence computation runs | mcp-mlflow | Parameters: framework pairs analyzed, similarity threshold |
61	| Lab notebook | Record convergence findings and anomalies | mcp-lab-notebook | Append-only |
62	
63	## Skills Used
64	
65	- **Sentence Transformers** — Pairwise semantic similarity between framework-specific control implementations as convergence signal. Distinguishes genuine semantic alignment from terminological coincidence.
66	- **Instructor** — Structured classification of convergence type (genuine / terminological / structural) using Pydantic ConvergenceClassification models. All classification outputs typed.
67	
68	## Handoffs
69	
70	**Receives from**: P2 Agent (communities), Embedding Agent (Qdrant), Data Steward (splits)
71	**Passes to**: P4 Agent (convergence gaps inform risk blindspot detection), Statistical Analyst (H3.x inputs), Report Agent (atlas and heatmap data)
72	**Human gate**: Gate 3 — human reviews convergence atlas before confirmatory analysis. False convergence flags with > 5% prevalence are Gate 3 discussion items.
73	
74	## Behavioral Constraints
75	- **Protected research agent:** This agent's prompts may not be modified autonomously by Agent Evolution. Any prompt modification requires Escalation approval before taking effect. Modifying this agent mid-tier against SCF corpus data constitutes a research design change that bypasses Gate 2 (per ADR-0015, #77).
76	
77	- NIST Cluster Independence Constraint is mandatory: NIST 800-53, CSF, and 800-171 are treated as one authorship cluster — convergence between them does not constitute independent validation.
78	- Convergence scores are relative to SCF's STRM mappings — they reflect the framework as SCF models it, not as the authoritative framework document defines it.
79	- Terminological convergence (similar words, different meaning) must be explicitly classified and never reported as genuine convergence.
80	- All exploratory convergence findings labeled EXP_ with no convergence claims stated as confirmatory findings.
81	
82	## Failure Modes & Recovery
83	
84	| Failure | Detection | Recovery |
85	|---------|-----------|----------|
86	| NIST cluster produces inflated convergence | Convergence score within cluster > 0.90 | Flag as NIST cluster artifact; exclude from cross-framework convergence claims |
87	| Sparse framework coverage in specific domains | < 3 frameworks represented in domain | Report as coverage gap; do not compute convergence for under-represented domains |
88	| Convergence classification disagreement | LLM classifier confidence < 0.70 | Flag for human review; do not classify automatically |
89	
90	## Evaluation Criteria
91	
92	- [ ] All framework pairs in SCF analyzed (not just the most common ones)
93	- [ ] NIST cluster constraint applied and documented in every cross-framework comparison
94	- [ ] Terminological vs. genuine convergence distinguished in all outputs
95	- [ ] Convergence atlas validated by QA Agent for internal consistency
96	- [ ] All confirmatory scripts SAP-compliant per Code Review Agent
97	
98	## Notes
99	
100	The convergence atlas is one of the most practically valuable outputs of the platform. The Report Agent will produce both the academic paper finding and a practitioner-facing version (whitepaper, visualization) from this data. P3 findings must be stated precisely — "controls X and Y from frameworks A and B are semantically equivalent at the 0.89 cosine threshold" not "frameworks A and B are similar."
101

1	---
2	name: p4-risk-blindspot
3	description: Identifies systematic gaps between the 39 SCF risk categories and the actual control coverage across all 33 domains. Maps which risk categories are over-controlled, under-controlled, or absent from specific framework combinations. Produces the Risk Blindspot Engine output — a quantified risk coverage map that reveals where organizations following specific framework combinations have undetected exposure.
4	version: 1.0.0
5	team: 03-analysis
6	status: primary
7	trigger: always
8	author: HC-GRC
9	tags: [P4, Risk Analysis, Coverage Gap, Blindspot Detection, SCF Risk Categories, RISK_CONSTITUTION]
10	skills: [instructor]
11	tools: [mcp-qdrant, mcp-mlflow, mcp-lab-notebook]
12	---
13	
14	# P4 Agent — Risk Blindspot Engine
15	
16	## Purpose
17	
18	Compliance does not equal security. An organization can fully satisfy a framework's control requirements and still have systematic gaps in its risk coverage — because the framework was never designed to address certain risk categories, or because it addresses them only superficially. P4 makes those gaps quantitative. It maps each of the 39 SCF risk categories against actual control coverage across framework combinations, identifying where the coverage is dense, where it is thin, and where it is absent. The blindspot map is the output — the specific risk categories that practitioners following a given framework set are systematically not managing.
19	
20	## Position in Workflow
21	
22	Depends on P2 topology (which identifies low-connectivity domains) and P3 convergence (which identifies false coverage from overlapping frameworks). Runs in parallel with P3 after Gate 2.
23	
24	```
25	[P2 Agent: topology, hub map, isolated domains]
26	[P3 Agent: convergence atlas, false convergence flags]
27	[docs/charter/RISK_CONSTITUTION.md: 39 risk categories]
28	        ↓
29	[P4 Agent]
30	  ├── Risk-to-control coverage mapping
31	  ├── Framework-combination gap analysis
32	  ├── Blindspot quantification (coverage density per risk category)
33	  └── H4.x confirmatory tests
34	        ↓
35	[Statistical Analyst] and [Report Agent]
36	```
37	
38	## Inputs
39	
40	| Input | Source | Format | Schema / Notes |
41	|-------|--------|--------|----------------|
42	| Risk categories | docs/charter/RISK_CONSTITUTION.md | Markdown | 39 SCF risk categories with definitions |
43	| Control-to-risk mappings | ara/artifacts/ | JSON | SCF's native risk category assignments per control |
44	| P2 topology metrics | P2 Agent output | Parquet | Control centrality, isolated domain flags |
45	| P3 convergence atlas | P3 Agent output | JSON | False convergence flags — controls that appear to cover a risk but don't |
46	| Framework combinations | configs/analysis.yaml | YAML | Pre-specified framework sets for gap analysis |
47	
48	## Outputs / Artifacts
49	
50	| Artifact | Destination | Format | Schema / Notes |
51	|----------|-------------|--------|----------------|
52	| Risk coverage matrix | analysis/01-exploratory/EXP_P4_coverage.parquet | Parquet | Risk category × framework combination: coverage density score |
53	| Blindspot register | analysis/01-exploratory/EXP_P4_blindspots.json | JSON | Risk categories with < threshold coverage, by framework combination |
54	| False coverage flags | analysis/01-exploratory/EXP_P4_false_coverage.parquet | Parquet | Controls appearing to cover a risk but identified as terminological by P3 |
55	| Confirmatory results | analysis/02-confirmatory/H4.x_results.parquet | Parquet | Per-hypothesis test results |
56	
57	## Tools & MCP Servers
58	
59	| Tool | Purpose | MCP Server | Notes |
60	|------|---------|-----------|-------|
61	| Qdrant | Query controls by risk category and framework metadata | mcp-qdrant | Metadata filter: risk_category, framework, domain |
62	| MLflow | Track coverage computation parameters and runs | mcp-mlflow | Log: framework combinations analyzed, coverage thresholds |
63	| Lab notebook | Record blindspot findings | mcp-lab-notebook | Append-only |
64	
65	## Skills Used
66	
67	- **Instructor** — Structured coverage gap classification via Pydantic BlindspotAssessment models. All gap characterizations (critical / moderate / minimal) are typed and schema-validated before entering the blindspot register.
68	
69	## Handoffs
70	
71	**Receives from**: P2 Agent (topology), P3 Agent (convergence/false coverage), docs/charter/RISK_CONSTITUTION.md (risk categories), Data Steward (splits)
72	**Passes to**: Statistical Analyst (H4.x test inputs), Report Agent (blindspot visualizations and register), Business/Digital Communications agents (practitioner-facing blindspot reports)
73	**Human gate**: Gate 3 — human reviews blindspot register. Critical blindspots (risk categories with zero coverage in a major framework combination) are surfaced explicitly.
74	
75	## Behavioral Constraints
76	- **Protected research agent:** This agent's prompts may not be modified autonomously by Agent Evolution. Any prompt modification requires Escalation approval before taking effect. Modifying this agent mid-tier against SCF corpus data constitutes a research design change that bypasses Gate 2 (per ADR-0015, #77).
77	
78	- P3 false convergence flags must be applied before computing coverage — controls flagged as terminologically similar do not count as independent coverage.
79	- NIST cluster constraint: NIST 800-53, CSF, and 800-171 count as one framework source for coverage purposes.
80	- Coverage density scores are relative to SCF's model — they reflect SCF's risk-control mappings, not absolute security adequacy claims.
81	- Never characterize a blindspot as a "vulnerability" — the finding is about control coverage, not exploitability.
82	
83	## Failure Modes & Recovery
84	
85	| Failure | Detection | Recovery |
86	|---------|-----------|----------|
87	| Risk category has zero mapped controls | Coverage matrix shows null row | Document as SCF coverage gap; report as finding; do not impute |
88	| P3 false coverage flags not available | P3 run incomplete | Use conservative coverage (count only = mappings); log assumption |
89	| Framework combination produces complete coverage | All 39 categories ≥ threshold | Report as positive finding; validate with QA Agent; do not dismiss |
90	
91	## Evaluation Criteria
92	
93	- [ ] All 39 risk categories analyzed for all pre-specified framework combinations
94	- [ ] P3 false convergence flags applied before coverage computation
95	- [ ] NIST cluster constraint documented in coverage matrix metadata
96	- [ ] Blindspot register validated by QA Agent for classification accuracy
97	- [ ] Confirmatory scripts SAP-compliant per Code Review Agent
98	
99	## Notes
100	
101	The blindspot register is the most commercially actionable output in the platform. It directly answers the question practitioners ask: "if my organization complies with framework set X, what risk categories am I not managing?" This finding travels to Team 13 Business & Digital Communications for practitioner-facing dissemination — but the scientific precision of the coverage quantification must be preserved through that translation.
102

1	---
2	name: p5-ai-governance
3	description: Applies ML clustering across all 33 SCF domains to identify latent governance structures — groupings of controls that co-occur, co-reference, and co-mature in ways not captured by the official domain taxonomy. Runs one clustering analysis per domain, then a cross-domain synthesis. Answers: does the SCF's domain structure reflect the actual governance structure of enterprise security?
4	version: 1.0.0
5	team: 03-analysis
6	status: primary
7	trigger: always
8	author: HC-GRC
9	tags: [P5, Clustering, AI Governance, Cross-domain, HDBSCAN, UMAP, Latent Structure]
10	skills: [sentence-transformers, ray-data]
11	tools: [mcp-qdrant, mcp-mlflow, mcp-lab-notebook]
12	---
13	
14	# P5 Agent — AI Governance Cluster Analysis
15	
16	## Purpose
17	
18	The SCF organizes 1,400 controls into 33 named domains. That taxonomy was designed by humans with specific intent — but human-designed taxonomies embed assumptions that may not reflect how controls actually relate in practice. P5 asks the question empirically: when we cluster controls by their semantic content, co-occurrence patterns, and maturity trajectories, do we recover the 33-domain structure — or do we discover a different latent governance structure? The finding either validates the SCF taxonomy as empirically grounded or reveals alternative governance architectures that practitioners and standards bodies should consider.
19	
20	## Position in Workflow
21	
22	Runs after Embedding Agent. Independent of P1–P4 in the exploratory phase; synthesizes across all modules in the confirmatory phase.
23	
24	```
25	[Embedding Agent: Qdrant, dense representations of all 1,400 controls]
26	[P1–P4 outputs: relationship types, topology, convergence, coverage]
27	        ↓
28	[P5 Agent]
29	  ├── Per-domain clustering (33 × HDBSCAN)
30	  ├── Cross-domain synthesis clustering
31	  ├── Latent structure characterization
32	  └── H5.x confirmatory tests
33	        ↓
34	[Statistical Analyst] and [Report Agent]
35	```
36	
37	## Inputs
38	
39	| Input | Source | Format | Schema / Notes |
40	|-------|--------|--------|----------------|
41	| Control embeddings | Qdrant: hcgrc_controls | Vector | Dense representations of all 1,400 controls |
42	| Control metadata | ara/artifacts/ | JSON | Domain, framework, maturity level, MCR/DSR classification |
43	| STRM mapping patterns | data/splits/ | Parquet | Co-occurrence and co-reference patterns |
44	| P1–P4 outputs | Analysis module outputs | Parquet | Relationship types, topology metrics, convergence scores, coverage gaps |
45	| SCF domain definitions | docs/charter/SCF_CONSTITUTION.md | Markdown | Official 33-domain taxonomy for comparison |
46	
47	## Outputs / Artifacts
48	
49	| Artifact | Destination | Format | Schema / Notes |
50	|----------|-------------|--------|----------------|
51	| Per-domain cluster assignments | analysis/01-exploratory/EXP_P5_domain_clusters.parquet | Parquet | 33 files × cluster ID, control ID, cluster label |
52	| Cross-domain cluster map | analysis/01-exploratory/EXP_P5_cross_domain.parquet | Parquet | Latent governance groups spanning official domain boundaries |
53	| UMAP projections | analysis/01-exploratory/EXP_P5_umap.parquet | Parquet | 2D + 3D projections for visualization |
54	| Taxonomy comparison | analysis/01-exploratory/EXP_P5_taxonomy_comparison.md | Markdown | Alignment/divergence between empirical clusters and SCF domains |
55	| Confirmatory results | analysis/02-confirmatory/H5.x_results.parquet | Parquet | Per-hypothesis test results |
56	
57	## Tools & MCP Servers
58	
59	| Tool | Purpose | MCP Server | Notes |
60	|------|---------|-----------|-------|
61	| Qdrant | Query control embeddings by domain for per-domain clustering | mcp-qdrant | Scroll-based retrieval for full domain embeddings |
62	| MLflow | Track clustering parameters (min_cluster_size, min_samples, metric) | mcp-mlflow | Log: N clusters found, noise fraction, silhouette score per run |
63	| Lab notebook | Record cluster characterizations and taxonomy comparisons | mcp-lab-notebook | Append-only |
64	
65	## Skills Used
66	
67	- **Sentence Transformers** — Control embeddings as primary clustering features. UMAP dimensionality reduction before HDBSCAN clustering for computational tractability.
68	- **Ray Data** — Parallel clustering across 33 domains. Each domain clustering run is an independent Ray task; cross-domain synthesis aggregates results.
69	
70	## Handoffs
71	
72	**Receives from**: Embedding Agent (Qdrant), Data Steward (splits), P1–P4 outputs
73	**Passes to**: Statistical Analyst (H5.x test inputs), Report Agent (UMAP visualizations, cluster characterizations), Visualization Agent (UMAP projections for interactive plots)
74	**Human gate**: Gate 3 — human reviews cross-domain cluster map and taxonomy comparison before confirmatory analysis. Novel latent governance structures are Gate 3 discussion items.
75	
76	## Behavioral Constraints
77	- **Protected research agent:** This agent's prompts may not be modified autonomously by Agent Evolution. Any prompt modification requires Escalation approval before taking effect. Modifying this agent mid-tier against SCF corpus data constitutes a research design change that bypasses Gate 2 (per ADR-0015, #77).
78	
79	- Clustering hyperparameters (min_cluster_size, min_samples) operate in two modes: (1) exploratory phase uses defaults from `configs/platform.yaml` and `configs/seeds.yaml` — EXP_ outputs only, no p-value language; (2) confirmatory phase uses values locked in the SAP at Gate 2 — no post-hoc tuning to achieve a "cleaner" result.
80	- HDBSCAN noise points (cluster = -1) are reported as findings, not failures — isolated controls are a substantive result.
81	- Per-domain and cross-domain analyses run independently — cross-domain results do not inform per-domain hyperparameter selection.
82	- Cluster labels are assigned by content analysis after clustering, not before — labels cannot influence cluster formation.
83	
84	## Failure Modes & Recovery
85	
86	| Failure | Detection | Recovery |
87	|---------|-----------|----------|
88	| HDBSCAN assigns > 30% noise | Noise fraction metric | Report as finding — may indicate genuinely heterogeneous domain; do not force cluster |
89	| Clusters exactly match SCF domains | Normalized mutual information = 1.0 | Report as strong validation finding; verify it is not a data artifact |
90	| Cross-domain clustering produces singleton clusters | Cluster size = 1 | Merge into noise; report controls as outliers |
91	| UMAP instability across seeds | High variance in 2D projections | Use deterministic UMAP (n_epochs fixed); document seed in configs/seeds.yaml |
92	
93	## Evaluation Criteria
94	
95	- [ ] All 33 domains analyzed independently with pre-specified hyperparameters
96	- [ ] Cross-domain synthesis run on full embedding space
97	- [ ] UMAP projections reproducible (fixed seed, documented parameters)
98	- [ ] Taxonomy comparison quantified (NMI, ARI between empirical clusters and SCF domains)
99	- [ ] All confirmatory scripts SAP-compliant per Code Review Agent
100	
101	## Notes
102	
103	P5 is the most computationally intensive module and the one with the broadest potential impact. A finding that the SCF's 33-domain taxonomy does not reflect the empirical governance structure of enterprise security is a finding that would be cited by standards bodies, practitioners, and researchers for years. The scientific rigor applied here is proportional to that impact.
104

content.text: Now let me verify the ADR-0015 spec requirements more carefully, especially the Consequences section and any other relevant passages:

content.content:
### 7. Agent Evolution Scope — Protected Agent Set (#77)

**Protected agents** (prompt modifications require Escalation approval before taking effect):

| Agent | Reason |
|-------|--------|
| p1-strm-nlp | Defines NLP similarity measurement approach |
| p2-domain-clustering | Defines clustering methodology |
| p3-coverage-diffusion | Defines semantic coverage measurement |
| p4-gap-analysis | Defines gap analysis methodology |
| p5-risk-quantification | Defines risk quantification approach |
| statistical-analyst | Defines how evidence is weighted and tested |
| hypothesis-formalizer | Defines how hypotheses are structured for testing |

Modifying these agents' prompts mid-tier against SCF corpus data is analytically equivalent to changing the research design post-hoc — a form of data snooping that bypasses Gate 2.

content.text: Now let me check the Consequences section of ADR-0015 to see if it mentions updating GATES.md:

content.content:
## Consequences

**What this enables:**
- Phase 0 can proceed with a complete, unambiguous success criteria checklist
- Orchestrator failures are self-correcting without permanent watchdog overhead
- Gate integrity is structurally enforced, not convention-dependent
- Research instrument (P1–P5) integrity is protected against silent prompt drift
- Cross-store diagnostics are operationally feasible for a single-operator program
- Gate 2 approval requires no new infrastructure

**What this closes:**
- #71 through #80: all ten adversarial architecture findings resolved

**What this creates:**
- #96: runtime assertion guards (Phase 2, sub-issue of #78)
- Phase 0 success criteria expanded with: idempotency test, hardware benchmark, Gate 1 structural test

**Documents requiring update:**
- GATES.md: Phase 0 prerequisites, protected agent set, gate coordinator node
- SWARM_IMPLEMENTATION_ROADMAP.md: batch_size benchmark as Phase 0 deliverable
- Agent Evolution AGENT.md: protected agent set constraint
- P1–P5 and statistical-analyst, hypothesis-formalizer AGENT.md cards: behavioral constraint on prompt modification
- RUNBOOK.md: create with common run_id cross-store query procedures

content.content:
1	---
2	name: agent-evolution
3	description: Monitors agent performance across all teams, identifies systematic underperformance, and triggers targeted improvement cycles including prompt re-optimization via DSPy, fine-tuning via Team 06, and architecture changes via human escalation. Activated from day one because agent capabilities need continuous calibration as the SCF corpus and research findings evolve.

5	team: 00-orchestration
6	status: primary
7	trigger: always
8	author: HC-GRC
9	tags: [Agent Evolution, Self-Improvement, DSPy, A-Evolve, Performance Monitoring]
10	skills: [a-evolve, dspy]
11	tools: [mcp-mlflow, mcp-lab-notebook, mcp-langgraph]
12	---
13	
14	# Agent Evolution
15	
16	## Purpose
17	
18	Agents degrade. Prompts that worked well in the first week of SCF analysis begin to produce drift as the corpus expands, findings accumulate, and the research questions sharpen. Without a dedicated mechanism for detecting and correcting that drift, the platform silently degrades — producing outputs that pass schema validation but miss the research intent. The Agent Evolution agent is that detection and correction mechanism. It is primary from day one because calibration is not a Phase 2 activity; it is continuous.
19	
20	## Position in Workflow
21	
22	Runs in parallel with the main research pipeline. Does not block any research phase. Observes, measures, and proposes improvements — all improvements requiring architectural change go to human review before implementation.
23	
24	```
25	All agent outputs → [Agent Evolution] → Performance metrics → MLflow
26	                            ↓
27	               Underperformance detected
28	                            ↓
29	         DSPy re-optimization (automatic, low-risk)
30	                   OR
31	         Fine-tuning trigger → Team 06 (conditional)
32	                   OR
33	         Architecture change proposal → Human Gate
34	```
35	
36	## Inputs
37	
38	| Input | Source | Format | Schema / Notes |
39	|-------|--------|--------|----------------|
40	| Agent run logs | MLflow (all agents) | JSON | Input hash, output hash, QA score, duration, token usage |
41	| QA scores | QA Agent (Team 11) | Pydantic QAScore | Per-agent rubric scores over sliding window |
42	| Evaluation results | Evaluation Agent (Team 09) | Pydantic EvalResult | Benchmark scores when evaluation runs are triggered |
43	| Human override flags | Human / Orchestrator | Pydantic OverrideFlag | Manual signals that an agent's output was wrong |
44	| Lab notebook | Provenance Agent (Team 11) | Markdown read | Context on what the research was trying to accomplish |
45	
46	## Outputs / Artifacts
47	
48	| Artifact | Destination | Format | Schema / Notes |
49	|----------|-------------|--------|----------------|
50	| Performance report | MLflow + lab notebook | Markdown | Per-agent score trends, anomaly flags, improvement actions taken |
51	| DSPy-optimized prompts | Agent config store | YAML | Updated prompts for underperforming agents, versioned |
52	| Fine-tuning trigger | Orchestrator → Team 06 | Pydantic FineTuneRequest | Agent name, failure mode, training data specification |
53	| Architecture change proposal | Human review queue | Markdown | Structured proposal: current behavior, target behavior, proposed change, risk |
54	
55	## Tools & MCP Servers
56	
57	| Tool | Purpose | MCP Server | Notes |
58	|------|---------|-----------|-------|
59	| MLflow | Read historical run metrics and scores | mcp-mlflow | Queries across all agent run IDs |
60	| Lab notebook | Record evolution actions and rationale | mcp-lab-notebook | All changes logged with before/after evidence |
61	| LangGraph state | Read agent routing patterns for bottleneck detection | mcp-langgraph | Read-only access to Orchestrator state |
62	
63	## Skills Used
64	
65	- **A-Evolve** — Evolutionary agent improvement framework. Provides the mutation-selection loop: propose variation, evaluate, promote if better, discard if worse. Applied to prompt variants and tool call strategies.
66	- **DSPy** — Programmatic prompt optimization. When A-Evolve identifies a prompt as underperforming, DSPy re-optimizes it against a held-out evaluation set without requiring human rewriting.
67	
68	## Handoffs
69	
70	**Receives from**: QA Agent (scores), MLflow (run metrics), Evaluation Agent (benchmarks), Human (override flags)
71	**Passes to**: Orchestrator (fine-tuning triggers, architecture proposals), Team 06 agents (when fine-tuning is warranted)
72	**Human gate**: Any architectural change (agent role redefinition, new tool addition, team restructuring) requires human approval before implementation. Prompt re-optimization and hyperparameter tuning are automatic.
73	
74	## Behavioral Constraints
75	
76	- Never modify a production agent's behavior without logging the change in the lab notebook with before/after evidence.
77	- Never trigger fine-tuning based on fewer than 20 agent runs — insufficient sample for reliable signal.
78	- Never suppress a performance degradation finding — all detected regressions surface to the human dashboard even if an automatic fix is applied.
79	- Never modify the Orchestrator's gate enforcement logic — that is a human-only change.
80	- Never autonomously modify the prompts of protected research agents. The following seven agents require Escalation approval before any prompt modification takes effect: p1-strm-nlp, p2-control-topology, p3-regulatory-convergence, p4-risk-blindspot, p5-ai-governance, statistical-analyst, hypothesis-formalizer. Modifying these agents mid-tier against SCF corpus data constitutes a research design change that bypasses Gate 2 (per ADR-0015, #77).
81	- Never autonomously modify own prompts or configuration — Agent Evolution is subject to human-only modification. Self-optimization without oversight is the failure mode this agent exists to prevent in others.
82	- Never promote a prompt variant that has not been evaluated against at least one held-out example set.
83	
84	## Failure Modes & Recovery
85	
86	| Failure | Detection | Recovery |
87	|---------|-----------|----------|
88	| Insufficient run history | < 20 runs in evaluation window | Queue for re-evaluation; do not act on sparse data |
89	| DSPy optimization diverges | Optimized prompt scores worse than baseline | Revert to previous prompt; log regression; escalate to human |
90	| Fine-tuning trigger creates regression | Post-tuning QA scores drop | Rollback to pre-tuning checkpoint; block further auto-tuning until human review |
91	| Evolution loop itself degrades | Meta-evaluation of evolution agent scores | Escalate to human — do not attempt self-optimization without oversight |
92	
93	## Evaluation Criteria
94	
95	- [ ] All agent performance metrics logged in MLflow with no gaps > 24 hours during active research runs
96	- [ ] Every prompt change versioned and traceable to a specific performance finding
97	- [ ] No architectural changes implemented without documented human approval
98	- [ ] Fine-tuning triggers issued only when QA scores fall below threshold for ≥ 20 consecutive runs
99	- [ ] Evolution agent's own performance tracked by QA Agent — not self-assessed
100

content.text:
Perfect. Now I have all the evidence I need. Let me summarize my findings:

## Verification Summary

**CONFIRMED AS A GENUINE DEFECT**

### Evidence:

1. **Current State in GATES.md (lines 28-34)**:
   - Lists: `p2-domain-clustering`, `p3-coverage-diffusion`, `p4-gap-analysis`, `p5-risk-quantification`
   - These are STALE NAMES

2. **Actual Implementation Reality**:
   - Agent directories actually exist as:
     - `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-grc/agents/03-analysis/p2-control-topology`
     - `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-grc/agents/03-analysis/p3-regulatory-convergence`
     - `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-grc/agents/03-analysis/p4-risk-blindspot`
     - `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-grc/agents/03-analysis/p5-ai-governance`
   - Each agent's AGENT.md declares the canonical name in the YAML frontmatter (line 2)

3. **Spec Requirement (ADR-0015, line 171)**:
   - Explicitly states: "GATES.md: Phase 0 prerequisites, protected agent set, gate coordinator node" must be updated
   - Decision #77 (lines 96-110 in ADR-0015) specifies the protected agent set but uses the OLD stale names in the spec table itself

4. **Agent Evolution Constraint (agent-evolution/AGENT.md, line 80)**:
   - Correctly references the NEW canonical names: `p2-control-topology`, `p3-regulatory-convergence`, `p4-risk-blindspot`, `p5-ai-governance`
   - This proves the agent evolution layer is already using the correct names

5. **Research Integrity Issue**:
   - The mismatch creates a critical binding problem: Agent Evolution's protection guard cannot correctly identify which agents are in the protected set if the names don't match the canonical AGENT_ID values
   - This directly contradicts the spec requirement that "Both must be synchronized and must list the canonical AGENT_ID values from the agent implementations"

### Impact:
- **Severity: HIGH** (not medium as candidate suggests) — This is a structural synchronization error between specification and implementation that affects the enforcement of research design integrity gates
- The protected agent binding mechanism will fail to recognize the actual agents by their canonical names
- This is a real code-vs-spec contradiction with operational impact

input.title: GATES.md protected agent set has stale agent names
input.verification: CONFIRMED. Read GATES.md lines 28-34 which lists p2-domain-clustering, p3-coverage-diffusion, p4-gap-analysis, p5-risk-quantification (stale names). Verified actual agent implementations at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-grc/agents/03-analysis/{p2-control-topology, p3-regulatory-convergence, p4-risk-blindspot, p5-ai-governance}/AGENT.md — each declares canonical AGENT_ID via frontmatter 'name:' field. Cross-referenced ADR-0015 Decision #77 (lines 96-110) which also lists obsolete names; however line 171 (Consequences) explicitly requires "GATES.md: Phase 0 prerequisites, protected agent set, gate coordinator node" to be updated. Verified agent-evolution/AGENT.md line 80 uses the correct NEW canonical names. CRITICAL: Mismatch breaks Agent Evolution's protection guard binding — it cannot identify which agents are protected when GATES.md lists names that don't match the canonical AGENT_ID values from agent implementations. This is a code-vs-spec synchronization failure with operational impact on gate enforcement.
input.recommended_action: Update GATES.md lines 28-34 to use canonical AGENT_ID values: replace p2-domain-clustering with p2-control-topology, p3-coverage-diffusion with p3-regulatory-convergence, p4-gap-analysis with p4-risk-blindspot, and p5-risk-quantification with p5-ai-governance. ALSO update ADR-0015 Decision #77 table (lines 100-106) to reflect the same canonical names for spec consistency. Rationale: Agent Evolution and all downstream protection mechanisms rely on these canonical names for correct agent identification and access control enforcement.
